title: Battery storage proves its value in moderating Texas power price volatility
gist: Wood Mackenzie's Ed Crooks reports that Texas's rapid battery storage buildout (857 MW to 21.7 GW since 2021) has suppressed ERCOT summer power price spikes despite rising gas prices and a growing renewables share, but this cannibalizes storage investors' own returns even as ERCOT peak demand is projected to more than triple by 2032 on data centre growth. The same newsletter issue also covers an escalating US-Iran conflict pushing oil and gas prices sharply higher, a new data centre moratorium in New York, and a PJM capacity auction reliability shortfall.

themes.theme: Texas Battery Storage Moderates Price Volatility

insights.claim: Texas battery storage capacity surged from about 857 megawatts in 2021 to about 21.7 gigawatts in 2026, making Texas the second-largest battery storage market in the US after California.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Rapid buildout signals a maturing storage market and warns of return compression as capacity keeps scaling.
insights.quote: Battery supply capacity on the ERCOT grid has risen from about 857 megawatts in 2021 to about 21.7 gigawatts this year.
insights.locator: Another proposed explanation for the moderation in price extremes

insights.claim: ERCOT wholesale power price peaks have moderated sharply since 2023: North zone average prices rose from $34/MWh in January to $123/MWh in July 2022, and from $25/MWh in January to $266/MWh in August 2023, but summer spikes have been much smaller in 2024, 2025 and so far in 2026.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A sharp reduction in extreme price spikes reduces the value proposition for merchant storage and peaker investments underwritten on historical volatility.
insights.quote: In 2022, average power prices in ERCOT’s North zone rose from US$34/MWh in January to US$123/MWh in July... In 2023, prices rose from US$25/MWh in January to US$266/MWh in August.
insights.locator: In the early 2020s, rising electricity demand and warm weather

themes.theme: Volatility Framework and the Cannibalization Problem

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's research attributes power price volatility to three principal factors: natural gas prices, the share of wind and solar generation on the grid, and the flexibility of thermal power generation.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Gives investors a repeatable model to forecast volatility regimes in any power market by tracking these three inputs.
insights.quote: Our research shows that power price volatility is driven principally by three factors: gas prices, the proportion of wind and solar on the grid, and the flexibility of thermal power generation.

insights.claim: Despite rising gas prices and a growing share of wind and solar (13% of ERCOT generation in 2021 to 19% in 2026) — both factors that should increase price volatility — realized volatility fell rather than rose since 2023, which Wood Mackenzie analyst Kasim Khan attributes primarily to increased battery storage availability.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A structural, non-weather explanation for suppressed volatility implies storage capacity is now large enough to cap tail-risk price events across a major US grid.
insights.quote: We would have expected a big increase in volatility since 2023, but instead we saw a decrease. The extreme prices have all but disappeared.

insights.claim: Battery storage in Texas is exhibiting cannibalization: by discharging during high-price periods and capping price spikes, the growing storage fleet is suppressing the very price volatility that generates its own investment returns.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Storage investors should expect declining per-unit returns as the fleet scales, even as absolute deployment keeps growing — a warning for late entrants underwriting deals on historical volatility.
insights.quote: The problem for investors, Khan says, is that by preventing extreme price spikes, the storage industry weakens its own returns. It is a phenomenon sometimes known as cannibalisation: new investments can eat the profits of the entire sector.

insights.claim: The Texas battery storage market has not yet become saturated, and investment returns for battery projects remain attractive even though they are lower than during the extreme-price years of 2022-2023.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Suggests a window remains for new storage investment in Texas before cannibalization fully erodes margins.
insights.locator: The Texas storage market does not appear to have become saturated yet

insights.claim: Local price effects at specific grid nodes can make an individual battery storage investment compelling even when average prices across the broader ERCOT market are declining.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Investors should underwrite storage deals on nodal/locational price data rather than system-wide averages, since node-level volatility can persist after broad averages compress.
insights.locator: Local price effects can also be very significant

themes.theme: ERCOT Demand Growth and the Data Centre Boom

insights.claim: ERCOT projected in April 2026 that peak power demand, including new data centres and other large loads, will rise from about 113 gigawatts currently to about 368 gigawatts by 2032.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A more than threefold demand increase — even if only partially realized — implies massive new generation and storage investment needs in Texas through the early 2030s.
insights.quote: ERCOT projected in April that its peak demand, including new data centres and other large loads, would rise from about 113 gigawatts this year to about 368 gigawatts in 2032.
insights.locator: Over the next few years, the demands placed on the storage industry

insights.claim: Texas is the number one US state and the number one region globally for proposed data centre development, according to Wood Mackenzie data.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Positions Texas as the epicenter of AI/data-centre-driven power demand growth, with knock-on implications for gas, renewables, and storage buildout there.
insights.quote: For proposed data centre development, Texas is the number one state in the US, and the number one region anywhere in the world, Wood Mackenzie data show.
insights.locator: For proposed data centre development, Texas is the number one state

insights.claim: Rising Texas summer power demand is being driven by population and economic growth, cryptocurrency mining, and data centres, with ERCOT supply expected to reach about 49 terawatt hours in July 2026 versus about 39 terawatt hours in July 2021.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Quantifies the multi-year demand growth trajectory underpinning the case for continued storage and generation investment.
insights.locator: Driven by population and economic growth, cryptocurrency mining and data centres

themes.theme: Market Design and Weather Risk to Storage Economics

insights.claim: ERCOT's energy-only market design, which pays generators only through wholesale price movements rather than capacity payments, combined with a relatively high share of variable renewable generation (about 11% wind and 8% solar in 2026), structurally amplifies wholesale price volatility and makes ERCOT an especially attractive market for battery storage investment.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Market design (energy-only vs. capacity markets) is a key variable for where storage economics work best — a lens for comparing ERCOT to PJM or other capacity-market grids.
insights.locator: There are features of ERCOT that make it particularly attractive

insights.claim: More frequent or intense heatwaves and storms would tend to increase power price volatility and thereby increase the returns to battery storage investments, while surging electricity demand that reduces spare grid capacity (as expected in Texas) could similarly boost storage's value.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Gives investors two forward-looking catalysts — extreme weather frequency and demand-driven capacity tightening — to watch as levers that could re-widen storage margins after cannibalization.
insights.locator: Other factors that can affect the economics of storage include extreme weather events

insights.claim: Escalating US-Iran conflict, including US missile and air strikes on Iranian targets and Iranian attacks on Gulf shipping and US bases, pushed Brent crude to about $87/barrel by mid-July 2026, up from about $72/barrel at the start of the month, while European TTF gas futures for August delivery rose to about €57/MWh from about €43/MWh over the same period.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A roughly 20% Brent rally and roughly 33% European gas futures rally in under three weeks signals material near-term inflationary and energy-security risk if the conflict escalates further.

insights.claim: Iran has asked its Houthi allies in Yemen to close the Bab El Mandeb Strait at the Red Sea entrance if Iranian power infrastructure is attacked, per Reuters, raising the risk of a second major shipping chokepoint disruption alongside the Strait of Hormuz.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A dual chokepoint threat (Hormuz plus Bab El Mandeb) would compound shipping and energy-supply disruption risk beyond what markets have priced for a single-strait scenario.
insights.locator: Iran has asked its Houthi allies in Yemen

themes.theme: Data Centre Policy Backlash and Grid Reliability

insights.claim: New York Governor Kathy Hochul imposed a one-year moratorium on new data centre environmental permits, aiming to develop a regulatory framework requiring new data centres to pay more for energy or supply their own power so they do not raise costs for household ratepayers.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Signals a growing policy trend of states shifting data-centre power costs onto developers rather than ratepayers — a regulatory risk factor for data centre site selection and capex planning.
insights.quote: in order to build a nation-leading regulatory framework that protects ratepayers, the environment, the energy grid and communities across the state

insights.claim: PJM's latest capacity auction left the market with a roughly 6.8-gigawatt shortfall below its 20% reserve margin target, which PJM attributed partly to a price cap on the auction that discourages new generation investment.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: A widening reserve margin shortfall in the largest US power market signals tightening reliability risk and potential upward pressure on capacity and energy prices in PJM territory.
insights.locator: PJM, the largest power market in the US, held another capacity auction

themes.theme: Electrification and US Gas Demand Outlook

insights.claim: IEA executive director Fatih Birol argued Europe's best path to energy security, given its inability to find major new oil and gas reserves, is rapid electrification, noting that 600 GW of completed European renewable projects — almost seven times the 85 GW added last year — could not deliver power due to insufficient grid capacity, which he called an economic crime.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Highlights grid interconnection, not generation build-out, as the binding constraint on European decarbonization — implicating grid/transmission investment as the next major capital allocation theme.
insights.quote: Last year, 85 gigawatts of renewables were added to the European grid. This is a big number. But 600 gigawatts, almost seven times that, of renewable projects finished [but could not supply power] because there was no grid to bring it to the homes and to the industry… This is really an economic crime.

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie's Horizons report forecasts US natural gas demand will nearly double from 2017 to 2035, driven by surging gas-fired power generation for data centres even as US LNG export capacity more than doubles over the same period.
insights.speaker: Ed Crooks
insights.so_what: Points to a structurally tightening US gas balance (domestic power demand plus LNG exports both rising) supporting the report's thesis that Henry Hub prices are set to rise.
insights.quote: Instead of simply absorbing supply, the US gas market is hungry for more.
